subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Particle Motion (Parametric)
stimulus:
For $t \geq 0$, a particle is moving along a curve so that its position at time $t$ is $(x(t),\, y(t))$. At time $t = 2$, the particle is at position $(1, 5)$. It is known that
$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{t}{e^{t+2}} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = \sin^2(t).$$

parts.prompt: Is the horizontal movement of the particle to the left or to the right at time $t = 2$? Explain your answer. Find the slope of the path of the particle at time $t = 2$.

scoring_points.description: Correct direction (to the right) with justification based on the sign of $dx/dt$

required_elements: $\dfrac{dx}{dt}\bigg|_{t=2} = \dfrac{2}{e^4} > 0$, so the particle is moving to the right

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point for moving to the right with reason. Because $\dfrac{dx}{dt}\bigg|_{t=2} = \dfrac{2}{e^4} > 0$, the particle is moving to the right at time $t = 2$.

sample_responses.response: $\dfrac{dx}{dt}\bigg|_{t=2} = \dfrac{2}{e^4} \approx 0.037 > 0$, so the particle is moving to the right.
sample_responses.commentary: Evaluates $dx/dt$ at $t = 2$, observes that it is positive, and correctly concludes rightward motion.

sample_responses.response: The particle is moving to the right.
sample_responses.commentary: States the correct direction but provides no justification based on the sign of $dx/dt$; the rubric requires a reason.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point for considering $\dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$. The slope of the parametric path is computed as the ratio of $dy/dt$ to $dx/dt$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the parametric slope formula and substitutes the given derivatives.
Inverts the ratio — the slope of the path $dy/dx$ is $dy/dt$ divided by $dx/dt$, not the other way around.

common_traps: Inverting the ratio to get $dx/dy$ instead of $dy/dx$

sample_responses.commentary: Correct numerical slope with three-decimal precision.
Reports $dy/dt$ as the slope instead of the ratio $dy/dt \div dx/dt$.

parts.prompt: Find the $x$-coordinate of the particle's position at time $t = 4$.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral expression for $x(4)$ using the initial condition $x(2) = 1$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point for integral. $x(4) = x(2) + \int_2^4 x'(t)\, dt$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly accumulates from the initial value $x(2) = 1$ using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus applied to $dx/dt$.
Uses wrong lower limit ($0$ instead of $2$) and omits the initial condition $x(2) = 1$.

required_elements: Correct numerical answer obtained by calculator

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point for answer (three-decimal precision required for calculator-evaluated integrals).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct numerical value to three decimal places obtained by numerical integration.
Decimal presentation error — AP Calculus requires three decimal places for calculator-evaluated answers.

parts.prompt: Find the speed of the particle at time $t = 4$. Find the acceleration vector of the particle at time $t = 4$.

common_traps: Reporting the velocity vector instead of its magnitude
Forgetting to take the square root

sample_responses.commentary: Uses the correct speed formula (magnitude of the velocity vector) and evaluates to three decimals.
Reports the velocity vector rather than its magnitude; speed is a scalar.

scoring_points.description: Correct acceleration vector $\langle x''(4),\, y''(4) \rangle$ at $t = 4$
common_traps: Reporting a scalar instead of a vector
Differentiating only $x'(t)$ and not $y'(t)$ for the acceleration
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point for acceleration vector. Acceleration $= \langle x''(4), y''(4) \rangle$.

sample_responses.commentary: Reports acceleration as a two-component vector with correct signed values to three decimals.
Reports a single scalar; acceleration in parametric motion is a vector with both $x$- and $y$-components.

parts.prompt: Find the distance traveled by the particle from time $t = 2$ to $t = 4$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the arc length integral for parametric motion.
Sums absolute values of the components rather than taking the magnitude of the velocity vector; arc length requires $\sqrt{(x')^2 + (y')^2}$.

required_elements: Correct numerical answer obtained by calculator

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point for answer (three-decimal precision required for calculator integration).

sample_responses.commentary: Numerical value to three decimals computed from the correct arc length integral.
Only two decimal places — AP scoring requires three decimals for calculator-computed values.
